entry.title: MSDS 6306 “Doing Data Science”
entry.content:
A tree showing my growth in doing data science.

From Overwhelmed to Growing Roots: My First Semester in Doing Data Science

I went into my first semester of a Master’s in Data Science knowing I’d be challenged—but I didn’t expect to enjoy the chaos as much as I did. Doing Data Science quickly became one of those classes that throws you straight into the deep end: massive datasets, unfamiliar concepts, and just enough direction to figure things out on your own. There were definitely moments of panic—like when my first project was late and I was convinced I had completely broken everything, only to discover the fix was… restarting my R session. But somewhere between debugging code, wrestling with messy data, and slowly making sense of concepts like hypothesis testing, I realized I wasn’t just surviving the course—I was actually starting to think like a data scientist. And that shift made all the difference.

What “Doing Data Science” Really Means

Going into the class, I thought “doing data science” would mostly mean cleaning data, running analyses, and maybe using tools like Python or SQL to get answers. Instead, the course completely reshaped that definition. There was no Python or SQL—we worked in R, which ended up being a really fun shift, and having a Python background actually gave me a helpful head start in thinking through problems. What surprised me most, though, was how much broader the experience was. We touched on machine learning, built interactive R Shiny apps, and spent a lot of time on something I hadn’t fully expected: professional communication. It wasn’t just about getting the right answer—it was about explaining it clearly, presenting it effectively, and making sure the story behind the data actually made sense to other people.

The Challenges (and the Branches I Had to Climb)

The challenges in this course were real, and not always in the ways I expected. Some of them were technical—like trying to work with massive datasets without bringing R to a halt, running into cryptic package issues like Arrow errors, or accidentally creating multiple versions of the same variable after a messy join and having to untangle what went wrong. Other times it was more conceptual, like trying to get a real intuition for p-values, hypothesis testing, and what uncertainty actually looks like in practice. And then there were the moments where everything just felt tangled—code wouldn’t run, outputs didn’t make sense, and I had to retrace my steps more than once to figure out where things went off. But in a way, those were the moments where I did the most branching out: every time something broke, I had to figure out how to fix it, and that’s usually where the real understanding started to grow.

Breakthrough Moments

One of the biggest breakthrough moments for me came during my first project on attrition, when I finally started to really understand sensitivity versus specificity—not just as definitions, but as trade-offs that actually matter. Before this project, I would’ve said I understood them, but I mostly just knew which formula went with which term and how to calculate them from a classification matrix. Working through the problem in context changed that completely. I could see how sensitivity captures how well a model identifies true positives, while specificity focuses on correctly identifying true negatives, and more importantly, how improving one often comes at the cost of the other. Once I saw that tension play out in my own analysis, it clicked in a much deeper way. It wasn’t just about calculating metrics anymore—it was about thinking carefully about what kind of mistakes were more acceptable, or more expensive, in a real-world setting.

The Coolest Things I Learned

Some of the coolest things I learned this semester were the skills that made me feel like I was actually starting to do data science, not just study it. I got comfortable wrangling messy, real-world data—cleaning it, transforming it, and creating features that actually mattered for analysis. I built visualizations that weren’t just decorative, but told a clear story about what was happening in the data, and I started to think more intentionally about how to communicate insights rather than just compute them. On the statistical side, concepts like hypothesis testing, permutations, and uncertainty went from abstract ideas to tools I could actively use to interpret results. I also learned how to work with time series data and recognize when patterns weren’t there, which is just as important as finding them. Looking back, I’m genuinely proud of how much I’ve picked up in a short amount of time—not because it was easy, but because I stuck with it long enough for things to start making sense!

What I’d Tell Myself (and Where I Am Now)

One of the biggest things I’ve learned this semester is that you don’t need to know everything at the start—you just need to know how to start. When I got stuck, my first move became doing a bit of research on my own, whether that was digging through documentation, finding explanations online, or using resources like Kaggle, Cross Validated, or Medium to see how other people approached similar problems. From there, it became much easier to ask better questions when I did reach out for help. I learned to lean on peers first, and then take those questions into office hours with my professor, which turned out to be one of the most valuable parts of the course. Even when I didn’t have something specific to ask, just having that time to talk through what I was learning helped me process everything more clearly and eventually brought up new questions.

What changed most over the semester wasn’t just my technical ability—it was my confidence. Having a cohort and professor who were going through the same challenges made a huge difference. There was a shared sense of figuring it out together, even when things were messy or uncertain. So– a big shoutout to my cohort: Diane, Lindsay, Josh, Aaron, Andy, Zach, Avery, Chase, Joshua, and Roger— we made it through the first semester, and the immersion conference was such a blast! I’m finishing this course not just with new skills, but with a real sense that I belong here, even though I’m still learning how to branch out.

entry.title: Protecting Health Data Beyond HIPAA
entry.content:
Your Fitness Tracker Collects Hospital-Grade Data. The Law Doesn’t Treat It That Way.

A fitness tracker with a mobile app.

If you wear a smartwatch or use a health app, you’re generating data that rivals what a hospital cardiac monitor produces — ECG readings, heart rate variability, blood oxygen levels, sleep staging, even stress scores. By some estimates, close to half of US adults now use health apps, and 440 million wearable devices shipped globally in 2024 alone.

Here’s the problem: almost none of that data is protected by HIPAA.

The Gap

HIPAA only applies to “covered entities” — hospitals, insurers, and clearinghouses — and their business associates. Consumer tech companies like Fitbit (now owned by Google), Oura, and Whoop don’t fall into those categories. That means your Apple Watch ECG and a hospital ECG can produce clinically indistinguishable data, but they receive fundamentally different legal treatment depending on who collected it.

This isn’t just a technicality. AI models running on wearable data can now infer depression risk, predict illness days before symptoms appear, and detect pregnancy from passive biometric signals. The sensitivity of the data has outpaced the legal framework designed to protect it.

What the FTC Is Doing

In the absence of HIPAA coverage, the FTC has stepped in through its Health Breach Notification Rule (HBNR). Originally adopted in 2009, the rule sat mostly dormant until 2021, when the FTC clarified that it applies to health apps and connected devices.

In 2024, formal amendments expanded the rule’s scope to explicitly name fitness trackers and wearables. A key change: sharing health data with ad networks without user consent now qualifies as a breach. The FTC backed this up with enforcement — GoodRx and the Premom fertility app both faced penalties in 2024–2025.

It’s a meaningful step, but the HBNR only covers breaches. It doesn’t establish broader privacy rights like access, deletion, or consent for data use.

Enter HIPRA

That’s where the Health Information Privacy Reform Act (HIPRA) comes in. Introduced in November 2025 by Senator Bill Cassidy, HIPRA is essentially “HIPAA for apps.” It would extend three categories of requirements to consumer health technology:

  • Privacy — consent before selling health data, minimum necessary standards, and rights to access, amend, and delete your information
  • Security — NIST-aligned safeguards comparable to what hospitals follow
  • Breach notification — standardized rules modeled on HIPAA, replacing today’s patchwork

HIPRA introduces a new data category called “Applicable Health Information” (AHI), which covers anything from heart rate data to AI-inferred health indicators. It’s still a bill, not law — but the direction is clear.

Meanwhile, AI Isn’t Waiting

While regulation catches up, healthcare AI is shifting from experimentation to execution in 2026. Ambient AI scribes are reducing physician documentation burden. Context-aware clinical decision support is replacing static drug-interaction alerts. Wearable data is being integrated directly into EHR workflows for chronic disease management.

All of this depends on data — and increasingly, on data that flows from consumer devices into clinical systems. As BCG notes, providers will use AI to analyze personal device data alongside genetic information and medical records to predict health problems before they start.

The promise is real. But so is the risk: the more health data AI consumes, the higher the stakes around who controls and protects it.

What You Can Do Now

Whether you’re building health tech, studying it, or just wearing a fitness tracker, here are a few practical steps:

  1. Audit your own data. Review the privacy settings and data-sharing permissions on your health apps and wearables. Disable features you don’t actively use.
  2. Read the privacy policy. Specifically look for language about third-party sharing, ad targeting, and research partnerships.
  3. Follow the regulatory trajectory. HIPRA, the updated FTC rule, and state-level consumer health data laws (like Washington’s My Health My Data Act) are shaping what compliance will look like in the next few years.
  4. Think about data governance early. If you’re working on health data projects, build privacy-by-design principles into your workflow now — don’t wait for regulation to force it.

The gap between how sensitive our health data is and how well the law protects it is closing. The question is whether it closes fast enough.
entry.summary: data science news of the week

entry.title: A Curious Mind (and Maybe a Little Monkey Business)

link.title: A Curious Mind (and Maybe a Little Monkey Business)

entry.content:
Buddy said put monkeys on your website.

A free monkey image.

If I had to describe myself in one image, it might just be a curious little monkey—wide-eyed, always reaching for something new, and maybe getting into a bit of mischief along the way. I’ve always been inquisitive by nature, the kind of person who wants to understand how things work, take them apart, and put them back together (hopefully better than before). There’s something playful in that process too—learning, experimenting, sometimes failing, but always moving forward.

Lately, that curiosity has found a new playground: machine learning. This semester, I’ve been diving into classification models and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), and it honestly feels like climbing through the branches of a really fascinating (and occasionally confusing) jungle. Each concept unlocks another layer—how models learn patterns, how they make decisions, and how we can enhance them with external knowledge to make them even more powerful.

There’s something incredibly exciting about seeing these ideas click. One moment you’re staring at equations and code that feel completely foreign, and the next, you start to recognize patterns and connections. It’s that spark—that “aha” moment—that keeps me coming back, just like a monkey reaching for the next branch.

Right now, I’m gearing up for my final exam, which feels a bit like preparing for a big leap. There’s a mix of excitement and nerves, but mostly I’m proud of how much I’ve learned in such a short time. No matter what happens, I know I’ve grown—and that I’m not done exploring yet.

So here I am: curious, playful, and always learning. Maybe a little like that monkey after all.
